title: Building Microservices
subtitle: Designing Fine-Grained Systems
author: Sam Newman
description: A practical guide to designing, building, and operating microservice architectures, covering everything from service decomposition to deployment, testing, and monitoring.
keyIdeas: Independent deployability is the core tenet of microservices
Service boundaries should align with business domain boundaries
Smart endpoints and dumb pipes keep services decoupled
Each service should own its data store to avoid shared-database coupling
Choreographed sagas manage distributed transactions without tight coupling
Observability (logging, metrics, tracing) is essential in distributed systems
Organizational structure should mirror service architecture (Conway's Law)
Evolutionary architecture allows services to change independently over time
keyTakeaways: Microservices enable independent deployability, scaling, and team autonomy
REST and messaging are the primary integration styles; choose based on coupling needs
Service decomposition should follow bounded contexts from Domain-Driven Design
Containers and orchestration platforms (Kubernetes) simplify microservice operations
Testing distributed systems requires a shift from end-to-end to contract testing
Monitoring a microservice architecture demands distributed tracing and centralized logging
Organizations must adopt DevOps culture for microservices to succeed
Start monolithic, then decompose as boundaries become clear — don't over-engineer prematurely
Sagas handle distributed transactions through compensating actions rather than two-phase commit
Serverless and event-driven architectures complement the microservices approach
whoShouldRead: Developers transitioning from monoliths to microservices
Technical leads wanting to understand microservice trade-offs
